solutions.solution: None is strong. If Francais are money, they should not be allowed to enter in this group. And if tears are not effective, then care should be taken very strictly instead of just banning at all.
None is strong. Non-violence is that strong a principle then we should not have any punishment because armed lacks proper relation (see step IV).
None is strong. I is rejected because it may not happen (will not follow). II is rejected because it is too simple and does not have arguments; substance.
Both are strong. Incentives do lure people. Second is also an established fact.

Second is strong. First is not very clear. If the state is secular, it means no government religion bias but not against religion as such. Second is in consonance with the prevailing notions of truth.

Both are weak. It is for the people to decide whether they want to get expensive food. A ban should not be enforced upon them. Second because it is not an acceptable truth. Nobody has said that country should be; means a country where nothing could be banned however harmful it is.
Both are weak. First is weak as it is not sure that including Films list would give respect to people's views. Second will not follow (or cannot be).
Second is strong. First is weak because it is not correct. (Is it the only way of thinking? Second is correct, on logical thinking.

Both are strong. They mention positive and a negative feature of merit clearly, hence these features are clearly harmful respectively and both are related with important aspects of the topic of mercy death.
Both are strong. On logical thinking, both look probable, both are desirable (harmful in case of second) and both touch significant aspects of it.
Second is strong. First is weak as you cannot abolish a system simply because it has loopholes and cracks. Second is an accepted truth.
None is strong. First is weak as it is based upon example. Second is not as it is debatable.
First is strong. First is an acceptable piece of truth backed by evidence. Second is weak as it is not true.
Both arguments strong. Both are true (both will follow). The first is really desirable while the second is really harmful.
Second is strong. First talks of a negative feature which does exist but is not sufficient enough to go for such a drastic action as disallowing for vote. Second is an established fact.
Second is strong. Affluent foreign tourists tend to prefer luxury hotels. First is debatable and, even if it is true, then what is the guarantee that criminals would not operate from other places?
Both are strong. Although both arguments contradict each other, yet both are based upon sound assumptions or facts and hence, independently, both are strong.
First is strong. Same explanation as in previous question.
I is strong as a subjective mode of selection is not desirable. II is of course, right.
None is strong. Temporary stopping of higher education will not hamper the nation's progress. It will reduce educated unemployment but so what? It will, then, increase uneducated unemployment.
Second is strong. First argument is debatable while the second is an established fact.
First is strong as it is based upon an established fact. Second is based upon example; hence it is a weak argument.

I is true as it is based on experiences. And political atmosphere in the campus is not really desirable as the campus is a place of learning, not politics. II is also true because tomorrow's leaders will come from today's students and it is good that they get some political training early.
Both are correct and strong arguments.

I is strong as it is true that atomic power works as deterrent to possible invaders that thus enhances sovereignty. II is not a strong argument as the imbalance would only be in India's favour and that won't be an undesirable thing.
Both are strong. Using computer does have the positive feature as mentioned in I but the negative feature is also there as mentioned in II.
Both are strong. Explanation same as in Q. 26.
None is strong. We do not 'waste money if we invest in computers. The second is based upon example.

I talks of a positive feature of 'absence of fight for power' in monarchy and this absence of fight is indeed, a desirable thing. II is a strong argument in the sense that it is in conformity with today's prevailing notions of truth.
Both are strong arguments.
Both are strong arguments.
None is strong. First is based on example while second is rejected in preliminary screening; it has no reasoning.
